author: Robert Carr
description: A hot turkey provolone sandwich with seared turkey cutlets, garlic-Dijon mayo, mushroom-onion pan sauce, roasted red peppers, dressed arugula, and toasted ciabatta or hoagie rolls.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1 1/2 pounds boneless skinless turkey breast cutlets, usually 4 cutlets
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 1/4 teaspoons Morton kosher salt, divided
1 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
8 ounces fresh cremini mushrooms, sliced 1/4 inch thick
1 (12-ounce) jar roasted red peppers, using 1/2 cup drained strips and 1 teaspoon pepper brine
4 ciabatta rolls or sturdy hoagie rolls, split
2 ounces baby arugula, about 2 packed cups
instructions: Move an oven rack 6 inches below the broiler and heat the broiler on high.
Line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil.
Pat the 1 1/2 pounds turkey breast cutlets very dry with paper towels.
If any turkey cutlet is thicker than 1/2 inch, place it between sheets of parchment or plastic wrap and pound it to an even 1/2 inch thickness.
Season both sides of the turkey cutlets with 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, 3/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, and 1/2 teaspoon dried thyme.
Sprinkle 1/4 cup all-purpose flour over a plate.
Press each seasoned turkey cutlet lightly into the flour on both sides, then shake off the excess flour so only a thin coating remains.

Make the garlic-Dijon mayo by stirring together 1/2 cup mayonnaise, 1 tablespoon Dijon mustard, 1 finely grated garlic clove, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon red wine vinegar, 1 teaspoon roasted red pepper brine, 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper in a small bowl.
Refrigerate the garlic-Dijon mayo until assembly.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium-high heat for 2 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil to the hot skillet.
Add as many floured turkey cutlets as will fit in a single uncrowded layer.
Cook the turkey cutlets for 2 to 3 minutes on the first side, until golden brown.
Flip the turkey cutlets with tongs and cook the second side for 2 to 3 minutes, until golden brown and the thickest part reaches 160 degrees F.
Transfer the cooked turkey cutlets to a plate.
If any turkey cutlets remain, add 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil to the skillet and repeat the 2 to 3 minutes per side cooking process with the remaining turkey cutlets.
Loosely cover the cooked turkey cutlets with foil while making the pan sauce.
Reduce the skillet heat to medium and add 1 tablespoon unsalted butter.
Add the 8 ounces sliced cremini mushrooms, the 1 thinly sliced yellow onion, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper.
Cook the mushrooms and onion for 7 to 8 minutes, stirring every minute, until the mushrooms are browned, the onion is softened, and most of the moisture has cooked off.
Stir in 2 minced garlic cloves and the remaining 1/2 teaspoon dried thyme.
Cook the garlic and thyme for 30 seconds, stirring constantly, until fragrant.
Add 1/2 cup low-sodium chicken broth, the remaining 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, and 1 teaspoon red wine vinegar.
Simmer the mushroom-onion pan sauce for 2 to 3 minutes, scraping the browned turkey bits from the skillet, until the liquid reduces by about half and coats the mushrooms and onions.
Return the turkey cutlets and any juices from the plate to the skillet.
Turn the turkey cutlets in the mushroom-onion pan sauce for 30 seconds so they are coated and hot.
Turn the heat to low so the turkey and mushroom-onion pan sauce stay warm while the rolls toast.
In a small bowl, stir together the remaining 2 tablespoons softened unsalted butter, the remaining 1 finely grated garlic clove, and 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Place the 4 split ciabatta rolls or hoagie rolls cut-side up on the foil-lined baking sheet.
Spread the garlic butter evenly over the cut sides of the 4 split rolls, using about 3/4 teaspoon garlic butter on each cut side.
Broil the buttered rolls for 1 to 2 minutes, watching constantly, until the cut sides are lightly toasted around the edges.
Move the 4 toasted top rolls to a plate and leave the 4 toasted bottom rolls cut-side up on the baking sheet.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-Dijon mayo on each toasted bottom roll.
Place 1 slice provolone cheese on each mayo-coated bottom roll.
Place 1 hot turkey cutlet on each bottom roll, folding or trimming the turkey as needed so it fits the bread.
Spoon about 1/4 cup mushroom-onion pan sauce over each turkey cutlet.
Add about 2 tablespoons drained roasted red pepper strips to each sandwich.
Tear the remaining 4 slices provolone cheese over the roasted red peppers, dividing 1 slice provolone per sandwich.
Broil the open sandwiches for 2 to 3 minutes, until the provolone is melted and lightly browned in spots.

While the cheese melts, toss 2 ounces baby arugula with the remaining 1 teaspoon red wine vinegar, 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Top each hot sandwich with about 1/2 cup dressed arugula.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic-Dijon mayo on each toasted top roll.
Close the sandwiches and serve immediately while the turkey, mushroom-onion pan sauce, and provolone are hot.
notes: This sandwich is hot by temperature, not spice; the flavor comes from browned turkey, mushroom-onion pan sauce, roasted red peppers, garlic-Dijon mayo, toasted bread, and melted provolone.
Turkey breast cutlets cook quickly and keep this recipe inside an hour.
Pounding any thick turkey pieces to 1/2 inch helps them cook evenly before the flour coating over-browns.
The thin flour coating gives the turkey better browning and helps the chicken broth reduce into a light pan sauce instead of tasting watery.
Use low-sodium chicken broth so the pan sauce can reduce without becoming too salty.
Roasted red pepper brine seasons the mayo with a little sweetness and acidity; use the liquid from the jar, not extra vinegar.
Do not crowd the turkey cutlets in the skillet. Crowding traps steam and prevents browning.
The turkey is pulled at 160 degrees F because it will stay hot in the skillet and under the broiler during assembly.
Dress the arugula right before serving so it stays peppery and fresh against the hot turkey and cheese.
Serve immediately while the bread is crisp, the turkey is hot, and the provolone is melted.
title: Hot Turkey Provolone Sandwiches
